narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate background, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection from our law school admissions process—it's a tough moment, and your determination to pursue law is clear. The automated system we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, focusing on key indicators like early and later academic rankings in school, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, family income level, and whether you'd be studying full-time. For you specifically, it considered your Year 1 school decile of 2 (well below the typical 5.7 average), Year 3 decile of 1 (also much lower than the usual 5.5), middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 35 (slightly under the 36.7 average), undergraduate GPA of 2.9 (below the 3.2 norm), and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. The model predicted a 61.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed our threshold for denial to ensure we admit students most likely to succeed on that critical test. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking—it stood out as unusually low compared to typical successful students, signaling potential challenges in sustaining strong performance later on. Your Year 1 decile also contributed somewhat toward the failure outlook, as it was similarly below average and hinted at early academic hurdles. On a positive note, your LSAT score had the strongest pull toward predicting bar exam success, showing solid reasoning skills that align well with passers, while your undergraduate GPA offered moderate support in the same direction. Your full-time study plan nudged things slightly toward success, matching the profile of most who pass, and your middle-income background had minimal impact either way. Overall, while you have strengths like your LSAT that compare favorably to averages, the low later-year rankings dominated the decision, setting you apart from the stronger trajectories of admitted students. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant with drive—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes those with consistent upward academic momentum. Moving forward, we encourage you to retake the LSAT if possible to boost that strength, focus on improving late-stage grades in any current program to lift those deciles, and consider part-time options or prep courses that have helped others in your position succeed on the bar. You're capable of turning this around; reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and keep applying—persistence pays off in law. We're rooting for your next steps.
